source.title: The neural encoding of self-generated and externally applied movement: implications for the perception of self-motion and spatial memory

confidence_rationale: Cullen's 2019 review directly addresses the neural mechanisms by which the brain distinguishes self-generated from externally-caused sensory signals, focusing on the vestibular and visual systems. The review describes the efference copy pathway in MSTd (medial superior temporal dorsal area) that enables visual reafference cancellation: self-generated optic flow (from the agent's own movement) is predicted by an efference copy of the motor command and subtracted, leaving only exafferent (externally-caused) visual motion. This is the exact biological substrate that MECH-101 maps to. The match is direct and mechanistically specific.
failure_signatures: If MSTd visual reafference cancellation uses body-state proprioception rather than scene-content-dependent optic flow as the prediction basis, the z_world_raw_prev input to ReafferencePredictor would not be necessary
If self-motion cancellation in visual cortex is complete and automatic (not requiring scene context), MECH-101's claim that z_world_raw_prev is needed would be undermined

mapping.source_claim_statement: MSTd neurons receive an efference copy of the motor command and use it to predict expected visual reafference (optic flow generated by the animal's own movement). This predicted reafference is subtracted from the incoming visual signal, canceling self-generated visual motion and leaving the neural response selective for externally-caused (exafferent) visual motion. The cancellation depends on the optic flow pattern (which encodes scene content) not just the body movement command.
mapping.ree_translation: The ReafferencePredictor in REE (SD-007) must take z_world_raw_prev as input -- the current world state -- to predict what visual change will result from the agent's action. This is necessary because in a local-view environment, locomotion brings new cell content into view: the predicted z_world change depends on what is adjacent in the world (available in z_world_raw_prev), not just on the body movement (available in z_self). Cullen's MSTd finding maps directly: the brain's reafference cancellation also requires scene-content-dependent optic flow prediction, not just body-state-dependent prediction.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Cullen describes the vestibular-visual interaction in MSTd in the context of 3D self-motion perception, which involves continuous ego-motion estimation in a rich visual environment. The REE grid-world presents a much simpler environment where locomotion effects on z_world are determined by which cells are adjacent. The biological reafference cancellation operates in continuous time on continuous sensory streams; the REE ReafferencePredictor operates on discrete step transitions on a structured latent. The principle (scene context is required for accurate reafference prediction) translates, but the implementation details do not map directly.
mapping.source_context: Review of neural encoding of self-generated vs. externally applied motion in vestibular nuclei, cerebellum, MSTd, and spatial memory circuits. Covers efference copy pathways, internal model predictions, and reafference cancellation across multiple sensory systems.

confidence_components.notes: Good source quality -- review in Frontiers Integrative Neuroscience from leading vestibular neuroscientist. Mapping fidelity is good because the principle (scene-content-dependent reafference prediction) maps directly to the z_world_raw_prev requirement in MECH-101. Transfer risk is lower than for other entries because the claim is mechanistically specific: the scene-state input requirement is a direct functional analog regardless of continuous vs. discrete implementation.
